speaker: interviewer
text: The date is August 15th, 1996. Survivor is Zalman Finkelstein, interviewer Naomi Rappaport. The city is Monticello, the state New York, the country USA, language English. My name is Naomi Rappaport, N-A-O-M-I R-A-P-P-A-P-O-R-T. And today is August 15th, 1996. I am conducting an interview with Mr. Zalman Finkelstein in Monticello, New York, USA.

speaker: interviewer
text: Good afternoon, Mr. Finkelstein.

speaker: interviewee
text: Good afternoon.

speaker: interviewer
text: And thank you very much for coming to tell us your testimony, and what happened to you during the Holocaust, and share this with the Shoah Foundation. We would like to begin the interview by asking you your name, and would you please spell it.

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is F-I-N-K-E-L-S-T-E-I-N, Z-A-L-M-A-N. Zalman.

speaker: interviewer
text: Zalman Finkelstein. And did you have-- was that your name when you were born?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you have any other names?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you have any nicknames?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did your mother call you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Zalman.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. When is your birth date? When were you born?

speaker: interviewer
text: 1968? Let me see if I have your birth date. You were born--

speaker: interviewee
text: Wait, wait a minute. Wait a minute. I'm a little foolish. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: That's OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: In July 18.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. And where were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Lodz.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how do you spell Lodz?

speaker: interviewee
text: Lodz, L-O-D-Z.

speaker: interviewer
text: And, and in what country is Lodz?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: Also, you were born in 1926. How old are you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I just in July 18, I finished 70 years.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. Can you tell me a little bit about Lodz? Where did you live in Lodz?

speaker: interviewee
text: What do you? Do you need the, the street?

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of house did you live in?

speaker: interviewee
text: We lived on the fifth floor in a big building.

speaker: interviewer
text: In an apartment building?

speaker: interviewee
text: Apartment building.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it your own building?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. It was a big, a big building, six floors high.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many people lived in the building?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, just on one floor, you had about 10 families living.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how many were there in your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, near the end, we were eight kids, and father and mother.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did your father do for a living?

speaker: interviewee
text: When I was young, about 8-9 years or whatever, he was a tool and die maker, an engineer. He finished engineering. And he had a helper in the beginning. But later on, he used to work for the Polish government. He used to make like this for rifles, tool, parts. At that time, they couldn't make it. They used buy the government from-- from Czechoslovakia.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he took it over. And he, he, he made the most of it for the government. And he made a good living out of it. But then his sister, she was in a-- in a better area, you know, richer people and things, so she had a restaurant, a big restaurant. So she have, why you have to work, with that and this and that, and support the Polish government. You know, they kill Jews, pogroms they made here and there in towns. So he said, yeah, good. I'll go with you. So this what-- That's what he did.

speaker: interviewee
text: He used to do.

speaker: interviewer
text: I asked that partially because I wanted to know if your father used your home to work from. Was it just for living, or for working as well?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. We were living.

speaker: interviewer
text: In the house?

speaker: interviewee
text: But downstairs was a little room downstairs, that he paid extra for it. And he used it.

speaker: interviewer
text: I see.

speaker: interviewee
text: Downstairs, in the back yard.

speaker: interviewer
text: I see. Did-- who lived in the building, all Jews or were there non-Jews in the building as well?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was non-Jewish too, but mostly Jewish. This was like, it was already-- you know, Hitler, Hitler made the ghetto, in this place, the blocks. Because the majority was Jewish, and it's concentrated.

speaker: interviewer
text: But when you were growing up in-- when you were born in 1926, you were still living in that. You were living in that place already.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. We were living in it.

speaker: interviewer
text: That was way before the ghetto.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, yeah, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Can you tell me, how many Jews lived in that area where you were? A lot of Jews or was it--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, a lot.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did most of them do there?

speaker: interviewee
text: You had-- you had a lot of business, a lot of manufacturers, textile, shoes, clothing, anything, stores, bakery. Was a life like heaven. Heaven. Was Christian store, but not too many, but were Christians. My mother had a good Christian, she used to come light the Shabbos, to cook a cup of coffee or whatever. So she would light it. And she was like a sister to mine, mine, mine mother.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have friends in the building who were not Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. I had one boy. One boy was friendly.

speaker: interviewer
text: And in general, what were the relationships between the non-Jews and the Jews who lived in your building or in the street?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. The only problems what my older brothers had, my brother with the owner. He was a German. You had a lot of Volksdeutsche in, in, in Poland, in Lodz particularly, a lot of Volksdeutsche. When the war broke out, he used to-- making with the light, with the mirror, making with the mirror to the airplanes.

speaker: interviewer
text: He used to signal.

speaker: interviewee
text: Signal them, I don't what he signaled, but he gave them some signs.

speaker: interviewer
text: Before the war broke out, did you visit these children's, these friends' homes? Did they come to your home, the non-Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, yeah, yeah, yeah, yeah. They used to come.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you play together?

speaker: interviewee
text: I said-- not too many. Because across the street was already antisemits. There was a-- was a store there. And she had a few kids, three or four kids. And those kids, they didn't like Jews. A matter of fact, when the Germans came in, her right away, swastika see every day-- with-- you know--

speaker: interviewer
text: Arm band.

speaker: interviewee
text: Arm band.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you personally ever bothered by non-Jewish children?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, a lot.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you ever hurt? In what way?

speaker: interviewee
text: A lot. I used to go to a school that I had to-- I had to walk a long way, a long way, 15, 20 minutes walk, good walk, running. And you pass by on the sidewalk, this was Christians, no Jews then this area. So they're always-- they're hiding in the-- in the-- in the-- in the building. And we used to go in a group, because we knew. So we used to go 10 kids, eight kids, you know, together. And then when we start seeing something already when you pass they hide inside, in the building. You know, they used to lock the doors at night, 11 o'clock they lock. Because there are drunks and things. So everybody-- the, the guy who cleaned the sidewalk, this part, he used to lock. If somebody worked, after 11:00, he ring the bell. And they used to pay him some money, so he came out from the house and opened the door.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we knew already where they hide. So we-- once we, we pass the bad part, we start running already. So then they jumped out, and they're after us. So they used to-- we used to fight. We used to get-- they used to have sticks and things. But this is what happened, oh, a lot of-- Did anybody ever protect you?

speaker: interviewee
text: No protection, no policemen. They always come-- they came always when was already finished. When it was finished, then they showed up, what happened here.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of school did you go to?

speaker: interviewee
text: I finished in Poland three, four classes, three or four.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of school was It

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a, a, a, a Jewish school.

speaker: interviewer
text: A Jewish public or--

speaker: interviewee
text: Public school, a public school.

speaker: interviewer
text: By Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: Jewish support, philanthropists, Jewish people.

speaker: interviewer
text: So did only Jewish kids go there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Only Jewish kids.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did they teach?

speaker: interviewee
text: They teach Hebrew. They teach general things, history; general things, everything. Everything what a normal school--

speaker: interviewer
text: And who were the teachers?

speaker: interviewee
text: Jewish people. My teacher was a woman. And I was always-- a funny thing, I don't know if I can say this-- but it was a funny thing. I-- she was very tall. I think-- I don't know today. She looks to me she was like all the way to the ceiling. But, you know, as a kid you cannot judge that right. But anyway, so I, I always lift up the hand to answer. You know, she used to write something on the-- on the wall, the thing. It was not-- it was a wall painted. It didn't even have what to write. Just a wall, and they paint this black. And then we used to write on it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I used to lift up, and want to answer. You sit. You sit there. You just answered before. Let somebody answer. So sometimes, again, you know something new came up. She walked over, lift me up by the ears, and you stay in the corner. [LAUGHS] Because I don't listen. Because you answer when, when I told you, OK. A nice lady. In what language were the classes held?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Polish, just Polish.

speaker: interviewer
text: Only Polish?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because they didn't allow it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were the teachers trained in Polish schools, or in Jewish schools?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. No, most, most was in, in Polish schools.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what about the, the-- your Hebrew studies? Did you go to cheder too?

speaker: interviewee
text: I had a rabbi home, coming. We had a rabbi in our building, Rabbi Worm. And he always sit outside on the-- on, on the steps, and talking to kids stories. You know?

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did he teach?

speaker: interviewee
text: Chumash, Gemara, you know, Tanakh.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did the orthodox children, the observant children go to the public-- the Jewish public school?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: They didn't.

speaker: interviewee
text: They were going to cheders and things. No, no. They didn't have nothing to do with the public schools.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your family's Jewish life like? Were you a Hasidic family?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. We were not Hasidic. But we were kosher and believed in the religion, believed in Shabbos, believed in yontif, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: How was your father dressed?

speaker: interviewee
text: Classic, the best you can see a person dressed. He was such a-- my mother the same thing, such a clean-- you could eat from the floor.

speaker: interviewer
text: But his clothes, were they different--

speaker: interviewee
text: Excellent.

speaker: interviewer
text: --than, than non-Jews?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: In what way?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know if the pants were different than non-Jews. But he was really dressed up, especially when he already start-- he left this, his work, and he went to work with the-- with the sister, in this huge restaurant. And this-- the best area was in Lodz, the best, this is.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the area?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is-- This was Piotrkowska, [NON-ENGLISH], all the way-- you know, Nowomiejska, you know, all those streets they were rich. [NON-ENGLISH], she had the restaurant.

speaker: interviewer
text: [NON-ENGLISH]?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's-- I can't even put my tongue--

speaker: interviewer
text: What area was that called? Did it have-- the whole area--

speaker: interviewee
text: They-- like the rich people. It's like over here, Fifth Avenue.

speaker: interviewer
text: And her restaurant's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was there.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the name of the restaurant?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. Big restaurant.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what did he wear, your father? What-- did he wear a kippah?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: He wore western clothes?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pants, a shirt, like me, you know, nice.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did he have a-- did he wear a hat all the time, or a yarmulke?

speaker: interviewee
text: He had a hat, he had a hat, not yarmulke. He wear the hat.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did he wear?

speaker: interviewee
text: A kapelusz.

speaker: interviewer
text: A kapelusz? That means like a fedora?

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, a kapelusz, like you wear a--

speaker: interviewer
text: A regular hat.

speaker: interviewee
text: A hat, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: That had to be steamed?

speaker: interviewee
text: A kapelusz, is like Polish.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes, no, I know what a kapelusz is, but there are different kinds of hats. And--

speaker: interviewee
text: No. He had it like the regular big hat.

speaker: interviewer
text: A regular, right. And your mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, she was just-- like hair.

speaker: interviewer
text: She had her own hair, or she wore a sheitel?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. Her hair.

speaker: interviewer
text: Her own hair.

speaker: interviewee
text: She was young.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was-- how were-- like you said Shabbos was important to your family. How was Shabbos celebrated?

speaker: interviewee
text: Excellent. Candlelight, fish of Shabbos, [NON-ENGLISH] every Shabbos, every Shabbos, [NON-ENGLISH] benched. Mother benched over the candles. And my father used to make a [NON-ENGLISH], normal. But they, they were not fanatics.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you sing zemirot at the end of a dinner?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. We didn't sing. But sometimes the kids start singing a song from school, Yiddish song.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah. Do you remember any of those songs at all?

speaker: interviewee
text: I have at home written.

speaker: interviewer
text: No, but can you remember to sing a little bit of a song for us?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I have the words, but I cannot put it together. I have a little booklet with me that I brought from the camp too. But yeah, we used to-- a little.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were the-- what were the songs about?

speaker: interviewee
text: About holidays, about freedom, about happiness, loving, you know, kissing each other, songs. Flowers in it.

speaker: interviewer
text: In what language?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Yiddish.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you sing together as a family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, kids together. Sometimes father used to say, you sing it. Let me see if you know it.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who taught you the songs? Were they taught in school or at home?

speaker: interviewee
text: We had in school songs. And those songs, we used to bring it home. And my father used to bring from restaurant, from over there, from other singers, you know, they used to sing [NON-ENGLISH], little short pieces, [NON-ENGLISH], used to come, a chazzen, you know. Used to come there, and [NON-ENGLISH] and this and that. [NON-ENGLISH] he used to sing. So he used to write it down, bring it home for Saturday, Friday night.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you don't remember any of them now?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I have it in my head.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, if you remember, we'll sing it later.

speaker: interviewee
text: But I-- I-- you know, it's, it's over 50 years, 55 years. You know, I think if would be normal situations, I think you would remember everything. Because you repeated.

speaker: interviewer
text: What language was spoken at home?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yiddish, Yiddish.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yiddish, and in general, was your family celebrating every single holiday?

speaker: interviewee
text: Every holiday.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did-- did you have a sukkah in your building or in your house?

speaker: interviewee
text: We used to-- we have this-- we had a-- we had a sukkah [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: A joint, a community sukkah.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, we had a--

speaker: interviewer
text: Who built it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Downstairs in, in, in the-- in the-- in the back yard, in the back. So we used to big [NON-ENGLISH]. So I don't remember, six families, five families together.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have to build it every sukkot?

speaker: interviewee
text: Every time, we couldn't leave it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Or did you-- did it stay there all year?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. You, you-- fresh, fresh. Because we're lucky that every time we went down that it's still there. Because some-- some kids-- not kids. Even grownups, they broke it, you know. They broke the wall, broke this down. They ripped the, the--

speaker: interviewer
text: The 'schach?

speaker: interviewee
text: The s'chach on top, you know. So--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did anyone sleep in the sukkah?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, nobody. They were afraid. Are you kidding?

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you-- Yeah. Did you eat in the sukkah?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. This we eat. Yeah, we ate there, sure, even it was broken. So we just quick put it back up, a little bit, and we showed the light and everything was there.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the holiday you liked most?

speaker: interviewee
text: Passover. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Why?

speaker: interviewee
text: They bought me new pants. I got new pants, a new jacket, new shoes, new socks.

speaker: interviewer
text: Everybody? Or just you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I know the young kids, they got-- the big brothers already-- I don't know what happened there. But they were dressed not bad.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who cooked in your house?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did she have help? You were a large family.

speaker: interviewee
text: No help. Some cook, some cook.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did she cook that you liked so much?

speaker: interviewee
text: Anything she cooked was out of this world. And my daughter-- you're going to meet my daughter, as sweetest person you ever can see, and she's number two from my mother.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: She give away everything what she has, didn't care.

speaker: interviewer
text: The-- in the building in which you lived, what was-- were there Orthodox Jews there, like Hasidic Jews, and people like you? How did-- how did you get along with the really orthodox community?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] In our house, in this building was not Hasidic Jews. I lived [NON-ENGLISH] 9, this was the street and the number of the building. In [NON-ENGLISH] 7, one building on the side there, was a wooden building. Over there you had 90% Hasidic, 90%.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: And a matter of fact, I still have a, a friend in Israel, although I saw him-- say, what year was it-- that I did a lot for this man in the camp. Well, I'm, I'm, I'm going to get off track in the camp. You needed a partner in the camps. If he finds something to eat, he shared with me. I, I organized something, they used to say organizing something. If I found something to eat, I didn't eat myself. I shared with him.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he came from that--

speaker: interviewee
text: And he came from [NON-ENGLISH], [NON-ENGLISH]. Now he came from a, a religious home. His father is a shochet and a rabbi. I used to go even to him to learn. And oh, religious, payos, you know. And the [NON-ENGLISH] payos. And we were kept together.

speaker: interviewer
text: But before the camp, how did the community get along as a kehillah?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not bad, not bad at all. It was very good. Because we all were like, even if you don't have the payos, but you eat. They used to say-- but they knew too what you're eating, even the whole building. They know each other, you know? So they knew what you're eating, who is your father, what's your father doing, how you feel. But everybody if not religious, they ate kosher food, they had good butchers.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about the secular Jews, the Bundists and Zionists? You mentioned that you were a member of Gordonia Habonim.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, Dror Habonim.

speaker: interviewer
text: Dror Habonim.

speaker: interviewee
text: Dror Habonim. This is already the later years, late, I started. They accept me already, you know, to continue.

speaker: interviewer
text: But, how did they get along?

speaker: interviewee
text: Completely excellent. Completely, no complaints. They didn't complain, the other side didn't complain. They just don't join the same group as you. You know, he sits all day and [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] My father had to go to work. So it's a-- it was a different-- today is the same thing here. You know, I helped out a few years ago, when I came. I was working on my own. You know, I was building, and I was fixing anything to houses.

speaker: interviewer
text: In the Monticello area?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Monticello area. I, I build a few bungalows, you know. So I, I made-- I made-- I made a good living. I made money. I came here as a professional. I didn't try this to do. I didn't want it. I knew this to do too, you know, woodwork. A matter of fact, I have a lot of the stuff from wood homemade. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: And you built for Hasidim?

speaker: interviewee
text: For Hasidim too, yes. Yes. I built for [NON-ENGLISH], and it changed. You want to an addition or something, I did. It's OK people. I have never had any problem--

speaker: interviewer
text: But there were no clashes or-- people got along?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. Not in Lodz. Not in towns.

speaker: interviewer
text: On a slightly different level, do you remember your bar mitzvah?

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't have a bar mitzvah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because the war broke out at that time.

speaker: interviewer
text: The war broke out in 1939, in September, and you were bar mitzvah in July? OK. The kehillah, the [NON-ENGLISH] or kehillah, whichever you choose to call it, who was in charge? Who was the macher in the kehillah?

speaker: interviewee
text: They have a person. He's a rabbi-like. He's the rabbi. He sits all the time in the kehillah, in the shul. And people coming, and he takes a, a, a, a, a, a [NON-ENGLISH] or takes a gemara out. And he explains this to people sitting around. And he translates from the Hebrew, whatever it's written in the Bible, the way it's written, he translates this in Yiddish.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he reads it, and then he says in Yiddish that everybody can understand. And he stays there, and gets a few dollars. This is a [NON-ENGLISH], or a-- just running the kehillah?

speaker: interviewee
text: The kehillah.

speaker: interviewer
text: I see-- who-- was there one kehillah? Or was-- Lodz was a big city. Were there different kehillahs?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sure.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who were usually the machers? The rich or the-- or the rabbonim?

speaker: interviewee
text: The rabbonim are poor.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: The machers is, is the people that they support the shul. They hold the shul. But rabbonim, normally they were poor people.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was the kehillah usually--

speaker: interviewee
text: Poor, because they just sit and study.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was the kehillah housed in the shul, or was it housed-- did it have its own building?

speaker: interviewee
text: They used to have separate. Sometime attached to the shul. A lot of them had attached. Richer, they had separate, completely away from it, away from it. But most was together, like separate. And they had-- they had in the shuls, poor shuls, they has classes in the shuls. After the davening--

speaker: interviewer
text: They studied Torah.

speaker: interviewee
text: They used to put together the-- the bank and this together, no chairs, you know, and sit there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your father was essentially an engineer, and a-- and a worker with-- was he involved in the trade union? Because Lodz was a textile trade union city, as well.

speaker: interviewee
text: No. He was not involved.

speaker: interviewer
text: Not involved. And the Bundist movement, was that--

speaker: interviewee
text: Maybe, yes. I don't know. But I think not. Maybe yes, you know. He doesn't give me, a kid, all the-- all the details. You know, what you know about it?

speaker: interviewer
text: I know you were only 13 before the--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --before Hitler came in to Poland. But you were young boy. What did girls and boys do? How did you amuse yourselves? Where did you go on Saturdays, Fridays? Were there movies?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it's-- if Friday night, we had to be everybody together having the dinner, and candlelight.

speaker: interviewer
text: And after the dinner?

speaker: interviewee
text: So everybody. Now if was time, we used to go downstairs in front of the building, and face talk a little bit, and that's it. Next day, across the street from us was a place. But we didn't do the Shabbos. We had to ride bicycles, especially for a few [NON-ENGLISH]. So they had bicycles. And you used to go up, and you know, like kids going around there. We used to be this. In wintertime, used to skating there, skates, and now a street. We used to go-- I had another friend that the father used to-- very nice people, they were. He used to sell horses, buy and sell it. Once a week was a big market from farmers coming, not far, in the same block but the end was a huge place. It was a market, and you could [INAUDIBLE] from the best you want. You know, and it was a good place, it was nice. And the area, you used to walk in the street. Summer used to go, it was a place where it was a manufacturer of bricks, used to make bricks. And, and over there was a huge lake. So we used to go a little swimming there, and this around it. All, all-- and even the lake was where Christians were going, and where the [NON-ENGLISH] going. This side [NON-ENGLISH], and this side goyim. Didn't go there. We were afraid. They had a better spot, you know. Was sand there. We had mud to go in. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: But we were OK. Once you start swimming, you don't care what in-- is on the bottom, but till you got to be a little deeper that you can start swimming, so you went till here in the mud, you know. So always this way that you were the secondary, you know. And you learned this. you learned this. Don't, don't, don't.

speaker: interviewer
text: We're going to have to change our tape now. We're going to change this tape.
